_comment: Source: Michigan MDHHS Section 8-10 Infection Control and Communicable Disease (Revised 6/27/2023, MDHHS Reviewed 2023). Base Michigan template — MCA-specific selections not configured.
protocol_reference: Michigan System Protocol Section 8-10: Infection Control and Communicable Disease — MDHHS Approved 6/27/2023
purpose: Govern infection control practices, personal protective equipment (PPE) requirements, and communicable disease exposure management for Michigan EMS providers. Universal precautions are the standard of care for all patient contacts. MCAs must have a written infection control plan and an exposure response program.

sections.title: Standard and Universal Precautions
sections.reference: Michigan Protocol 8-10: Infection Control — Universal Precautions
points: Universal precautions must be applied on ALL patient contacts — assume all blood and body fluids are potentially infectious
Minimum PPE for ALL patient contacts: gloves
PPE based on exposure risk:
— Gloves: all patient contacts
— Eye protection (goggles/face shield) and mask: risk of splash or spray of blood/body fluids
— Gown: risk of splash or extensive contact with blood/body fluids
— N95 or higher respirator: airborne precaution situations (suspected TB, COVID-19, measles, varicella)
Hand hygiene: wash hands or use alcohol-based hand sanitizer after every patient contact and after glove removal

sections.title: Airborne Precautions — Enhanced PPE
points: N95 (or higher) REQUIRED for: suspected or confirmed tuberculosis (TB), COVID-19, measles, varicella, SARS, MERS, or other airborne-transmitted disease
Place a surgical mask on the patient when airborne pathogen is suspected and patient can tolerate it
Place patient in negative pressure transport area if equipped; otherwise maximize ventilation (open windows) in patient compartment
High-flow nasal oxygen (HFNO) and nebulized medications generate aerosols — use N95 and face shield during these interventions
Full PPE (N95, face shield, gown, gloves) for intubation and other aerosol-generating procedures

sections.title: Post-Exposure Procedures
points: NEEDLE STICK or BLOOD/BODY FLUID EXPOSURE: immediately wash exposed area with soap and water (skin) or flush mucous membranes with water for 15 minutes
Report exposure to supervisor immediately and follow MCA/employer exposure response protocol
Complete MDHHS or employer-required exposure report forms
Seek medical evaluation promptly — post-exposure prophylaxis (PEP) for HIV, hepatitis B, and other pathogens must be initiated within 2 hours for maximum effectiveness
MCAs must have a designated post-exposure response program including access to occupational health or emergency department evaluation

sections.title: Vehicle and Equipment Decontamination
points: Decontaminate all equipment and patient compartment surfaces after patient contacts involving blood, body fluids, or suspected communicable disease
Use EPA-registered hospital-grade disinfectant per manufacturer instructions
Single-use items: discard appropriately — do NOT reuse single-use equipment
Sharps containers: fill to no more than 3/4 full before sealing and disposing per biohazardous waste regulations
Remove soiled linens and equipment immediately; bag and label biohazardous materials

deterioration_flags: Needle stick or blood/body fluid exposure — wash immediately; report to supervisor; seek medical evaluation within 2 hours for PEP consideration
Suspected or confirmed airborne pathogen (TB, COVID-19, measles) — ensure N95 is on; place mask on patient; maximize ventilation
scope_notes: Universal precautions: gloves on ALL patient contacts
N95 required: TB, COVID-19, measles, varicella, aerosol-generating procedures
Post-exposure: wash immediately; report to supervisor; seek medical eval within 2 hours
PEP for HIV/hepatitis B must begin within 2 hours of exposure
MCA must have written infection control plan and post-exposure program
Decontaminate all surfaces and equipment after blood/body fluid contact
